title: Compulsory Schooling Age and Early Labour Market Outcomes: Evidence from Hungary
abstract: We study a Hungarian reform that cut the compulsory schooling age from 18 to 16 and find sizeable effects on school completion and early labour market outcomes, especially for lower-SES students and the Roma minority.
summary_en: In 2012 Hungary cut the compulsory schooling age from 18 to 16. Using administrative data, this paper shows the reform raised dropout rates, reduced secondary-school completion (especially among lower-income and Roma students), and lowered employment and earnings in the first years after school. The effects are larger for groups already at risk of exclusion.
data_used: Hungarian administrative education records and matched employer-employee data, covering cohorts exposed and unexposed to the 2012 compulsory-schooling-age reduction.
policy_relevance: Direct Hungarian policy evidence: lowering the school-leaving age from 18 to 16 did not save money in any meaningful sense — it shifted costs downstream through worse employment outcomes, concentrated on the students the education system was already failing. Relevant for any future discussion of compulsory schooling age, vocational tracking, and early exit routes. Ministry of Human Capacities / education policy.
